---
title: "Basis and weight-class spreads to CME GF feeder futures"
subtitle: "Weekly Clovis cash − CME GF feeder-cattle settle · 700–799 lb Steers is the strict-hedging basis the futures contract tracks"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Read this first — what the chart shows by default.** The chart **defaults to strict basis only**: the single line that is *basis* in the strict hedging sense — Clovis cash for **700–799 lb Steers** minus the CME GF settle. That's the population the [CME GF Feeder Cattle futures contract specification](https://www.cmegroup.com/markets/agriculture/livestock/feeder-cattle.contractSpecs.html) tracks (Medium-and-Large Frame #1 and #1-2 muscle thickness Steers, 700–899 lb). Click the **Show: All weights & classes** button to expose the full 12-line view: lighter-weight Steers (calf-feeder spread), Heifers (steer-heifer-discount + weight spread), and 800–899 lb Steers (slight discount to the index target). The wider view is informative for cattle-cycle and cross-hedge thinking, but only the strict-basis line is directly hedgeable. See [Methodology → Basis derivation](methodology/basis.qmd) for the formal distinction.
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Caveat before scrolling.** Strict basis (700–799 lb Steers) is the gap between local cash and the relevant futures contract for the **same animal type the contract specifies** — what a producer hedging actual 700-lb steers realizes when they offset a futures position with a cash sale. The wider weight-class spreads on this chart are price-level relationships between calves or heavier feeders and the 750-lb-feeder futures benchmark — useful for understanding the cattle cycle and for cross-hedge thinking, but with much higher year-to-year variance than strict basis. The chart shows the historical record; it is not a forecast.
:::

## About this page

A producer who hedges 700–799 lb feeder steers on the CME — or a producer who reads futures as a forward-price reference for the same population — needs to know the **strict basis**: the typical gap between local Clovis cash for that weight-and-class combination and the futures settle on the same date. Lock in futures + sell cash gives you `settle + basis`, and a producer who knows their typical basis at week 22 for 700–799 lb Steers selling against the May contract has a defensible expected price for that scenario. The default chart view answers exactly that question — single line, single bin, strict basis.

For producers and Extension peers wanting a fuller picture of price-level relationships across the feeder-cattle cycle, the **Show: All weights & classes** toggle exposes the 11 additional cross-weight cash–futures spreads (5 lighter Steers bins, 5 Heifers bins, plus the 800–899 lb Steers heavier-than-index bin). Those values are real and informative — they reveal how calf-feeder and steer-heifer spreads move through the cycle — but they are *not* directly hedgeable basis, because the futures contract specification doesn't cover those populations.

The chart shows `cash − CME GF feeder-cattle settle` for three contract conventions: the **May contract** (the canonical spring reference, frequently used by spring-marketing producers), the **November contract** (the canonical fall reference, frequently used by stockers exiting grass), and the **Nearby contract** (whichever traded contract is closest to expiry on each auction date). Each line is the average across all years in the selected window, computed per week of the calendar year (1–52). The shaded band behind each line is the **min/max range** — the worst and best value observed for that week-of-year across the available years.

Four button rows above the chart let you switch the **show selector** (top: Strict basis only / All weights & classes), the **mode** (Nominal $ / Real December-2025 $), the **contract month** (May / November / Nearby), and the **year window** (bottom: All / Last 5 years / Last 3 years / latest calendar year). Each row maintains its own selection independently — at any moment, the chart shows the combination of all four highlighted choices. Real-dollar basis is computed per row as `basis_nominal × deflator(month_of_auction)` because both cash and futures settle scale by the same monthly CPI factor; see [Methodology → Basis derivation](methodology/basis.qmd) for the derivation.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: data-load

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

PROCESSED = Path("..") / "data" / "processed"
CPI_PATH = PROCESSED / "cpi_latest.parquet"
BASIS_PARQUET = PROCESSED / "clovis_basis_latest.parquet"
BASIS_CSV = PROCESSED / "clovis_basis_latest.csv"

basis_is_placeholder = False
data_source_label = None

if BASIS_PARQUET.exists():
    try:
        df = pd.read_parquet(BASIS_PARQUET)
        data_source_label = "Clovis cash − CME GF settle (combined Era B + MARS)"
    except Exception:
        df = None
else:
    df = None

if df is None and BASIS_CSV.exists():
    df = pd.read_csv(BASIS_CSV)
    data_source_label = "Clovis cash − CME GF settle (CSV fallback)"

if df is None:
    # Synthetic stand-in
    import numpy as np
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=2026)
    rows = []
    bins = [(300, 400), (400, 500), (500, 600),
            (600, 700), (700, 800), (800, 900)]
    for year in range(2018, 2027):
        for week in range(1, 53):
            for cl in ("Steers", "Heifers"):
                for lo, hi in bins:
                    for c in ("MAY", "NOV", "NEARBY"):
                        d = pd.Timestamp(f"{year}-01-01") + pd.Timedelta(weeks=week-1)
                        rows.append({
                            "auction_date": d,
                            "class": cl,
                            "frame": "Medium and Large",
                            "muscle_grade": "1",
                            "weight_break_low": lo,
                            "weight_break_high": hi,
                            "price_avg_cash": 200 + rng.normal(0, 10),
                            "n_lots": 5,
                            "contract_month": c,
                            "settle": 205 + rng.normal(0, 10),
                            "settle_date": d,
                            "basis": rng.normal(-5 if c == "NOV" else -3, 8),
                            "vintage": pd.Timestamp("2026-04-25"),
                        })
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    basis_is_placeholder = True
    data_source_label = "synthetic stand-in (no live basis data available)"
    print("SYNTHETIC STAND-IN BASIS DATA — for layout only.")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: data-shape

# Filter to the chart's standard population: grade-1 Medium-and-Large
# Steers and Heifers, 300-899 lb, with non-null basis. Add iso-week
# of the auction date for the per-week-of-year aggregation.

import pandas as pd

work = df[
    (df["class"].isin(["Steers", "Heifers"]))
    & (df["frame"] == "Medium and Large")
    & (df["muscle_grade"] == "1")
    & (df["basis"].notna())
].copy()
work["auction_date"] = pd.to_datetime(work["auction_date"])
work = work[
    (work["weight_break_low"] >= 300)
    & (work["weight_break_low"] < 900)
].copy()

work["iso_week"] = work["auction_date"].dt.isocalendar().week.astype(int)
work.loc[work["iso_week"] == 53, "iso_week"] = 52
work["weight_bin"] = work.apply(
    lambda r: f"{int(r['weight_break_low'])}–{int(r['weight_break_low']) + 99} lb",
    axis=1,
)

date_min = work["auction_date"].min()
date_max = work["auction_date"].max()
n_weeks = work["auction_date"].dt.date.nunique()
n_obs = len(work)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: deflate-basis

# CPI deflation for basis. Because basis is a difference of two prices
# observed in the same period, both sides deflate by the same monthly
# CPI factor:
#
#     cash_real_M    = cash_nominal_M    * deflator_M
#     settle_real_M  = settle_nominal_M  * deflator_M
#     basis_real_M   = cash_real_M - settle_real_M
#                    = (cash_nominal_M - settle_nominal_M) * deflator_M
#                    = basis_nominal_M * deflator_M
#
# So the per-row deflation is just basis × deflator(month_of_auction_date).
# Basis nominal is the original; basis_real adds the row-level multiplier.
# Same December 2025 basis month and same fallback-to-1.0 behavior as the
# cash charts.

import sys as _sys

BASIS_PERIOD = pd.Timestamp("2025-12-01")
cpi_basis_label = "December 2025"

deflation_available = False
if CPI_PATH.exists():
    cpi = pd.read_parquet(CPI_PATH)[["period", "cpi_u"]].copy()
    cpi["period"] = pd.to_datetime(cpi["period"])
    basis_rows_cpi = cpi[cpi["period"] == BASIS_PERIOD]
    if not basis_rows_cpi.empty:
        cpi_basis_value = float(basis_rows_cpi["cpi_u"].iloc[0])
        deflation_available = True
    else:
        fallback = cpi.sort_values("period").iloc[-1]
        cpi_basis_value = float(fallback["cpi_u"])
        cpi_basis_label = pd.to_datetime(fallback["period"]).strftime("%B %Y")
        deflation_available = True

if deflation_available:
    work["period_month"] = work["auction_date"].dt.to_period("M").dt.to_timestamp()
    cpi_lookup = cpi.set_index("period")["cpi_u"]
    work["cpi_u_month"] = work["period_month"].map(cpi_lookup)
    work["deflator"] = cpi_basis_value / work["cpi_u_month"]
    missing_cpi = int(work["deflator"].isna().sum())
    if missing_cpi:
        print(
            f"[deflate-basis] {missing_cpi} basis rows lack a CPI month-match; "
            "leaving nominal.", file=_sys.stderr,
        )
    work["deflator"] = work["deflator"].fillna(1.0)
    work["basis_real"] = work["basis"] * work["deflator"]
else:
    # No CPI available — fall back so the toggle is harmless.
    work["basis_real"] = work["basis"]
```

::: {.callout-important}
**Data source**: `{python} data_source_label` ·
**Window**: `{python} date_min.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')` to `{python} date_max.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')` (`{python} n_weeks` distinct auction weeks) ·
**Observations**: `{python} f"{n_obs:,}"` per-week-bin-class basis rows across three contract conventions ·
**Grade shown**: Medium and Large, muscle grade 1 ·
**Basis convention**: cash − settle on auction date (prior-trading-day fallback for weekend auctions, up to 5 days) ·
**Deflation basis (real-$ view)**: `{python} cpi_basis_label` CPI-U NSA (`CUUR0000SA0`).

The window's upper bound is bounded by **CME-settle availability**, not by the cash data alone. The Clovis cash parquet typically extends a week or two further than the latest available CME settle; auctions for which no settle is found within the 5-day fallback have a null `basis` and don't appear in this chart. The data catalog reports the cash-side window (which can run further); this page reports the basis-with-non-null-settle window.
:::

## Weekly basis pattern

Each line is the **average basis** for one weight class at each week of the calendar year, averaged across every year in the selected window. The shaded band behind each line is the **min/max range** observed across years for that week-of-year — the worst and best basis seen at that calendar position. Where the line sits inside a wide band, basis at that week is volatile year-over-year; where the band is tight, the historical pattern is reliable.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "Basis chart for the Clovis, NM cash auction relative to CME GF feeder-cattle futures. Two stacked panels (Steers above, Heifers below) plot basis ($/cwt; cash minus futures settle) against an auction-date timeline. Each weight bin (300-399 through 800-899 lb) is one line; the 700-799 lb Steers line is the strict basis (matches the CME GF contract specification), while the others are weight-class cash-futures spreads shown as cycle context. Three button rows above the chart toggle contract month (May, November, Nearby), year window, and currency mode."

import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots


def _agg_weekly(work_df, contract, basis_col):
    """Per (class, weight_bin, iso_week): min / mean / max of the chosen
    basis column (``basis`` for nominal, ``basis_real`` for CPI-deflated)."""
    s = work_df[work_df["contract_month"] == contract]
    if s.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame(columns=[
            "class", "weight_bin", "iso_week",
            "basis_min", "basis_avg", "basis_max", "n",
        ])
    return (
        s.groupby(["class", "weight_bin", "iso_week"], as_index=False)
         .agg(
             basis_min=(basis_col, "min"),
             basis_avg=(basis_col, "mean"),
             basis_max=(basis_col, "max"),
             n=(basis_col, "count"),
         )
    )


# Year windows — same vocabulary as the other chart pages.
_anchor = work["auction_date"].max()
_anchor_year = int(_anchor.year)
YEAR_WINDOWS = [
    ("all",    "All",            work["auction_date"].min(),                   _anchor),
    ("5y",     "Last 5 years",   _anchor - pd.DateOffset(years=5),             _anchor),
    ("3y",     "Last 3 years",   _anchor - pd.DateOffset(years=3),             _anchor),
    ("latest", f"{_anchor_year}", pd.Timestamp(_anchor_year, 1, 1),            _anchor),
]
CONTRACTS = ["MAY", "NOV", "NEARBY"]

# Match the other chart pages' bin colors so a producer switching pages sees
# consistent visual identity.
COLOR_BY_BIN = {
    "300–399 lb": "#6a3d9a",
    "400–499 lb": "#000000",
    "500–599 lb": "#2ca02c",
    "600–699 lb": "#d17a00",
    "700–799 lb": "#1f77b4",
    "800–899 lb": "#8b0000",
}
BIN_ORDER = [
    "300–399 lb", "400–499 lb", "500–599 lb",
    "600–699 lb", "700–799 lb", "800–899 lb",
]

# Semi-transparent versions of each bin color for the min-max band fill.
def _alpha(hex_or_rgb: str, a: float) -> str:
    h = hex_or_rgb.lstrip("#")
    if len(h) == 6:
        r, g, b = int(h[0:2], 16), int(h[2:4], 16), int(h[4:6], 16)
        return f"rgba({r},{g},{b},{a})"
    return hex_or_rgb
BAND_BY_BIN = {wb: _alpha(c, 0.12) for wb, c in COLOR_BY_BIN.items()}
INVISIBLE = "rgba(0,0,0,0)"

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=2, cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True,
    vertical_spacing=0.10,
    subplot_titles=(
        "NM Clovis Steers — Basis to selected contract ($/cwt)",
        "NM Clovis Heifers — Basis to selected contract ($/cwt)",
    ),
)


def _add_bin(agg_df, cls, wb, row, visible, show_legend):
    """Three traces per (class, bin): invisible min-line, max-line that
    fills 'tonexty' to create the band, then the average line on top."""
    sub = (
        agg_df[(agg_df["class"] == cls) & (agg_df["weight_bin"] == wb)]
        .sort_values("iso_week")
    )
    if sub.empty:
        # Three placeholder traces so visibility-mask indices stay aligned
        for _ in range(3):
            fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=[], y=[], showlegend=False, visible=visible),
                          row=row, col=1)
        return
    x = sub["iso_week"].astype(int).tolist()
    ymin = pd.to_numeric(sub["basis_min"], errors="coerce").astype(float).tolist()
    ymax = pd.to_numeric(sub["basis_max"], errors="coerce").astype(float).tolist()
    yavg = pd.to_numeric(sub["basis_avg"], errors="coerce").astype(float).tolist()
    n = pd.to_numeric(sub["n"], errors="coerce").astype("Int64").tolist()
    color = COLOR_BY_BIN[wb]
    band = BAND_BY_BIN[wb]

    # 1. min line — invisible, anchors the band's lower edge
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=x, y=ymin, mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=INVISIBLE, width=0),
        showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip", visible=visible,
        name=f"{wb} min",
    ), row=row, col=1)
    # 2. max line — fills 'tonexty' down to min, semi-transparent band
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=x, y=ymax, mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=INVISIBLE, width=0),
        fill="tonexty", fillcolor=band,
        showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip", visible=visible,
        name=f"{wb} max",
    ), row=row, col=1)
    # 3. avg line — solid, with full hover info
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=x, y=yavg, mode="lines+markers",
        line=dict(color=color, width=2),
        marker=dict(color=color, size=4),
        name=wb, legendgroup=wb,
        showlegend=show_legend, visible=visible,
        customdata=list(zip(ymin, ymax, n)),
        hovertemplate=(
            f"<b>{wb} · {cls}</b><br>"
            "Week %{x}<br>"
            "Avg basis: $%{y:.2f}/cwt<br>"
            "Range: $%{customdata[0]:.2f} → $%{customdata[1]:.2f}<br>"
            "n years: %{customdata[2]:,}<extra></extra>"
        ),
    ), row=row, col=1)


# Three-dimensional view space:
#   year (4) × contract (3) × mode (2 = Nominal, Real) = 24 view-states.
# Per state: 6 bins × 2 classes × 3 traces = 36 traces.
# Total: 24 × 36 = 864 traces.
#
# Trace emission order is locked so visibility-mask indices are predictable:
#   for year_idx in range(4):
#       for contract_idx in range(3):
#           for mode_idx in range(2):       # 0 = nominal, 1 = real
#               for cls in (Steers, Heifers):
#                   for wb in BIN_ORDER:    # 6 bins
#                       (3 traces: min-anchor, max+band, avg)
# Group index g = year_idx * 6 + contract_idx * 2 + mode_idx
# First trace of group g is at fig.data[g * 36].
MODES = ["nominal", "real"]
MODE_BASIS_COL = {"nominal": "basis", "real": "basis_real"}
# Show dimension — filters which (class, weight_bin) traces are visible
# WITHIN an active (year, contract, mode) group. "strict" surfaces only
# the canonical 700–799 lb Steers traces (3: min-anchor, max+band, avg);
# "all" surfaces every trace in the group (36 = 6 bins × 2 classes × 3).
SHOW_OPTIONS = ["strict", "all"]
N_SHOWS = len(SHOW_OPTIONS)
# Trace local-indices within a (year, contract, mode) group, per Show value:
#   strict → 700–799 lb is BIN_ORDER[4], Steers (cls_idx=0).
#            Local indices: 4*3 + 0, 4*3 + 1, 4*3 + 2 = 12, 13, 14.
#   all    → all 36 local indices.
STRICT_LOCAL_INDICES = [12, 13, 14]
N_TRACES_PER_GROUP = 36
N_CONTRACTS = len(CONTRACTS)
N_MODES = len(MODES)
N_GROUPS = len(YEAR_WINDOWS) * N_CONTRACTS * N_MODES
N_TRACES = N_GROUPS * N_TRACES_PER_GROUP

for y_idx, (y_key, y_label, lo_d, hi_d) in enumerate(YEAR_WINDOWS):
    sub_year = work[(work["auction_date"] >= lo_d) & (work["auction_date"] <= hi_d)]
    for c_idx, contract in enumerate(CONTRACTS):
        for m_idx, mode in enumerate(MODES):
            basis_col = MODE_BASIS_COL[mode]
            agg = _agg_weekly(sub_year, contract, basis_col)
            # Initial trace visibility must match the default 4-D state
            # (year=0, contract=0, mode=0, show=0=strict). show=strict
            # restricts visible traces to the Steers × 700-799 lb bin
            # (BIN_ORDER[4]); everything else starts hidden. Without this
            # narrowing the page would load with all 36 traces of the
            # (0,0,0) group visible — and the explicit y-axis range
            # would clip the wider weight-class-spread lines, producing
            # the visual bug "Strict basis is highlighted but all 12
            # lines are showing" until the user clicks any button.
            in_default_group = (y_idx == 0 and c_idx == 0 and m_idx == 0)
            for cls, panel_row in (("Steers", 1), ("Heifers", 2)):
                for bin_idx, wb in enumerate(BIN_ORDER):
                    is_default = (
                        in_default_group
                        and cls == "Steers"
                        and bin_idx == 4  # 700-799 lb is BIN_ORDER[4]
                    )
                    # Show legend on every Steers-row avg trace across all
                    # year×contract×mode groups. Plotly auto-hides legend
                    # entries for invisible traces, so the legend always
                    # reflects whichever group is currently visible.
                    show_legend = (cls == "Steers")
                    _add_bin(agg, cls, wb, row=panel_row,
                             visible=is_default, show_legend=show_legend)


def _group_idx(year_idx: int, contract_idx: int, mode_idx: int) -> int:
    return year_idx * (N_CONTRACTS * N_MODES) + contract_idx * N_MODES + mode_idx


def _visibility_for(year_idx: int, contract_idx: int, mode_idx: int,
                    show_idx: int = 0) -> list[bool]:
    """Build a 864-trace visibility mask for the given 4-D state.

    show_idx 0 (strict) restricts visibility to the 700-799 lb Steers
    traces only — strict basis under the CME GF contract spec. show_idx
    1 (all) surfaces every (class, weight_bin) trace in the active
    (year, contract, mode) group.
    """
    out = [False] * N_TRACES
    start = _group_idx(year_idx, contract_idx, mode_idx) * N_TRACES_PER_GROUP
    if show_idx == 0:  # strict basis only
        for li in STRICT_LOCAL_INDICES:
            out[start + li] = True
    else:  # all weight classes
        for i in range(start, start + N_TRACES_PER_GROUP):
            out[i] = True
    return out


def _yrange_for_state(year_idx: int, contract_idx: int, mode_idx: int,
                      show_idx: int = 0) -> list[float]:
    """Compute the y-axis range from the visible (year, contract, mode,
    show) data.

    Plotly's auto-range fails when many traces use ``fill="tonexty"``
    bands — it defaults to its empty range $-1 to $4 even though the
    visible traces have real values spanning much wider. Setting the
    range explicitly per state restores the correct view.

    For show=strict the range is computed from the strict-basis subset
    (Steers × 700-799 lb only), giving a much tighter y-axis (~ -$30 to
    +$10) that doesn't waste vertical space on the non-strict spreads.
    """
    _, _, lo_d, hi_d = YEAR_WINDOWS[year_idx]
    contract = CONTRACTS[contract_idx]
    basis_col = MODE_BASIS_COL[MODES[mode_idx]]
    sub = work[
        (work["auction_date"] >= lo_d)
        & (work["auction_date"] <= hi_d)
        & (work["contract_month"] == contract)
    ]
    if show_idx == 0:  # strict basis only — filter to Steers × 700-799 lb
        sub = sub[
            (sub["class"] == "Steers")
            & (sub["weight_break_low"] == 700)
        ]
    if sub.empty or sub[basis_col].dropna().empty:
        return [-30.0, 10.0] if show_idx == 0 else [-50.0, 50.0]
    y_min = float(sub[basis_col].min())
    y_max = float(sub[basis_col].max())
    pad = max((y_max - y_min) * 0.05, 1.0)
    return [y_min - pad, y_max + pad]


# Four button rows; per the cross-clearing pattern used on the cash
# chart pages, clicking any button sets a complete (year, contract,
# mode, show) state and clears the highlights on the other three rows.
# Each button represents the chosen dimension's value combined with the
# other three dimensions' BASELINE (year=All, contract=MAY, mode=Nominal,
# show=strict). The independent-toggle JS layer below restores the
# multi-row highlighting after each click.
#
# Updatemenus indices in the args dicts:
#   updatemenus[0] → contract row
#   updatemenus[1] → year row
#   updatemenus[2] → mode row
#   updatemenus[3] → show row

NOMINAL_Y_TITLES = {
    "yaxis.title.text": "Basis, $/cwt (nominal)",
    "yaxis2.title.text": "Basis, $/cwt (nominal)",
}
REAL_Y_TITLES = {
    "yaxis.title.text": f"Basis, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
    "yaxis2.title.text": f"Basis, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
}
MODE_Y_TITLES = {0: NOMINAL_Y_TITLES, 1: REAL_Y_TITLES}

# Row 0 — contract buttons. Default state baseline includes show=strict.
# (annotations[6] = heifers-empty-notice; visible=True for all three
# non-show rows because their cross-clearing fallback resets show→strict.)
contract_buttons = []
for c_idx, contract in enumerate(CONTRACTS):
    label = {"MAY": "May contract", "NOV": "November contract",
             "NEARBY": "Nearby contract"}[contract]
    yr = _yrange_for_state(0, c_idx, 0, 0)
    contract_buttons.append(dict(
        label=label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(0, c_idx, 0, 0)},
            {
                **NOMINAL_Y_TITLES,
                "updatemenus[1].active": -1,  # clear year row
                "updatemenus[2].active": -1,  # clear mode row
                "updatemenus[3].active": -1,  # clear show row
                "yaxis.range": yr,
                "yaxis2.range": yr,
                "annotations[6].visible": True,  # heifers-empty-notice ON
            },
        ],
    ))

# Row 1 — year window buttons. Default state baseline includes show=strict.
year_buttons = []
for y_idx, (y_key, y_label, lo_d, hi_d) in enumerate(YEAR_WINDOWS):
    yr = _yrange_for_state(y_idx, 0, 0, 0)
    year_buttons.append(dict(
        label=y_label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(y_idx, 0, 0, 0)},
            {
                **NOMINAL_Y_TITLES,
                "updatemenus[0].active": -1,  # clear contract row
                "updatemenus[2].active": -1,  # clear mode row
                "updatemenus[3].active": -1,  # clear show row
                "yaxis.range": yr,
                "yaxis2.range": yr,
                "annotations[6].visible": True,  # heifers-empty-notice ON
            },
        ],
    ))

# Row 2 — mode buttons. Default state baseline includes show=strict.
mode_buttons = []
for m_idx, mode in enumerate(MODES):
    label = "Nominal $" if mode == "nominal" else f"Real ({cpi_basis_label} $)"
    yr = _yrange_for_state(0, 0, m_idx, 0)
    mode_buttons.append(dict(
        label=label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(0, 0, m_idx, 0)},
            {
                **MODE_Y_TITLES[m_idx],
                "updatemenus[0].active": -1,  # clear contract row
                "updatemenus[1].active": -1,  # clear year row
                "updatemenus[3].active": -1,  # clear show row
                "yaxis.range": yr,
                "yaxis2.range": yr,
                "annotations[6].visible": True,  # heifers-empty-notice ON
            },
        ],
    ))

# Row 3 — show buttons. Default = Strict basis only.
# These two labels are intentionally distinguishable from the Mode row's
# labels (which all contain "$") — the JS layer disambiguates the two
# 2-button rows by label content.
# annotations[6] (heifers-empty-notice) toggles per show: visible when
# strict, hidden when all (because Heifers panel populates in "all" mode).
show_buttons = []
for s_idx, s_label in enumerate(("Strict basis only", "All weights & classes")):
    yr = _yrange_for_state(0, 0, 0, s_idx)
    show_buttons.append(dict(
        label=s_label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(0, 0, 0, s_idx)},
            {
                **NOMINAL_Y_TITLES,
                "updatemenus[0].active": -1,  # clear contract row
                "updatemenus[1].active": -1,  # clear year row
                "updatemenus[2].active": -1,  # clear mode row
                "yaxis.range": yr,
                "yaxis2.range": yr,
                # heifers-empty-notice: ON for strict (s_idx=0), OFF for all (s_idx=1)
                "annotations[6].visible": (s_idx == 0),
            },
        ],
    ))


# Initial y-range matches the default-active state (year=0, contract=0,
# mode=0, show=0=strict). Because show=strict filters to the 700-799 lb
# Steers panel only, the initial y-range is the much tighter "strict
# basis" range rather than the wide weight-class-spread range.
_initial_yrange = _yrange_for_state(0, 0, 0, 0)

fig.update_layout(
    height=1040,
    # Top margin needs to clear: subplot title (just above each panel),
    # legend (above subplot title), and FOUR button rows (above legend):
    #   y=1.56 — Show (Strict basis only / All weights & classes)
    #   y=1.44 — Mode (Nominal $ / Real $)
    #   y=1.32 — Contract (May / November / Nearby)
    #   y=1.20 — Year window (All / Last 5y / Last 3y / latest)
    # Legend sits at y=1.035; subplot titles between legend and panels.
    margin=dict(t=340, b=60, l=80, r=30),
    hovermode="x unified",
    paper_bgcolor="white",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    font=dict(family="Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif", size=13, color="#222"),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.035, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
    # Preserve subplot_titles (auto-added by make_subplots as layout
    # annotations) by appending our row-label annotations rather than
    # replacing the list.
    #
    # Final annotation indexing after this list is built:
    #   [0] Steers subplot title (from make_subplots)
    #   [1] Heifers subplot title (from make_subplots)
    #   [2] <b>Show</b> row label
    #   [3] <b>Mode</b> row label
    #   [4] <b>Contract</b> row label
    #   [5] <b>Year window</b> row label
    #   [6] Heifers-empty-notice (visible only when show=Strict basis only;
    #       toggled per state via the buttons' args + the JS applyState)
    annotations=list(fig.layout.annotations) + [
        dict(text="<b>Show</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.56, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
        dict(text="<b>Mode</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.44, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
        dict(text="<b>Contract</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.32, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
        dict(text="<b>Year window</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.20, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
        # Heifers-empty-notice — explains why the Heifers panel is blank
        # in strict mode (strict basis is Steers-only by definition; the
        # CME GF futures contract specification does not cover heifers).
        # Default visible=True (page loads in strict mode by default).
        # Visibility is toggled per state by the show buttons and the JS
        # applyState layer.
        dict(text=(
                "<i>Strict basis is Steers-only — switch to "
                "<b>'All weights &amp; classes'</b> "
                "to see Heifers cash–futures spreads.</i>"
             ),
             showarrow=False,
             xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.5, y=0.20,
             xanchor="center", yanchor="middle",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#666"),
             bgcolor="rgba(245, 245, 245, 0.85)",
             bordercolor="#cccccc",
             borderwidth=1,
             borderpad=10,
             visible=True),
    ],
    updatemenus=[
        dict(  # contract row (index 0)
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=0,
            x=0.18, y=1.32,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=contract_buttons,
        ),
        dict(  # year row (index 1) — All highlighted by default
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=0,
            x=0.18, y=1.20,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=year_buttons,
        ),
        dict(  # mode row (index 2) — Nominal highlighted by default
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=0,
            x=0.18, y=1.44,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=mode_buttons,
        ),
        dict(  # show row (index 3) — Strict basis only highlighted by default
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=0,
            x=0.18, y=1.56,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=show_buttons,
        ),
    ],
)

# X-axis = ISO week 1-52
fig.update_xaxes(
    tickmode="linear", tick0=1, dtick=1,
    tickfont=dict(size=9),
    showgrid=False, linecolor="#888", mirror=False, ticks="outside",
    range=[0.5, 52.5],
)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Week of calendar year", row=2, col=1)

# Y-axis = basis $/cwt; reference line at zero so positive/negative basis
# is visually obvious. Range is set explicitly because Plotly's auto-range
# defaults to its empty range when many traces use fill="tonexty" bands —
# bug we hit on weekly-trends with date axes, here it bites bands too.
fig.update_yaxes(
    showgrid=True, gridcolor="#e5e5e5",
    tickprefix="$", linecolor="#888", mirror=False, ticks="outside",
    title_text="Basis, $/cwt (nominal)",
    zeroline=True, zerolinewidth=1, zerolinecolor="#888",
    range=_initial_yrange,
)
fig.update_layout(yaxis2=dict(matches="y"))

fig.show()


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Independent-toggle JS layer.
#
# Plotly updatemenus are stateless — each button click sets fixed args, so
# the only built-in way to keep four button rows visually consistent is the
# cross-clearing pattern (clicking row N clears the highlights on rows
# !=N). This script post-processes the rendered chart to keep all four
# rows independently highlighted. It pre-computes every (year × contract ×
# mode × show) state's visibility mask + y-range as a JSON lookup table,
# listens for ``plotly_buttonclicked`` events, and re-applies the combined
# state via ``Plotly.update`` — including re-asserting ``active`` on all
# four menus so each row keeps its last selection.
#
# Disambiguating the two 2-button menus (Mode and Show): button count is
# 2 for both rows, so the JS handler distinguishes them by the FIRST
# button's label — Mode's first button is "Nominal $" (contains "$");
# Show's first button is "Strict basis only" (no "$"). The check is
# robust to future label tweaks as long as Mode labels keep their "$".
#
# Falls back gracefully: if the JS doesn't load, the buttons still work
# in the existing cross-clearing mode (their args are unchanged).

import json
from IPython.display import HTML, display

_state_table = {}
for _y in range(len(YEAR_WINDOWS)):
    for _c in range(N_CONTRACTS):
        for _m in range(N_MODES):
            for _s in range(N_SHOWS):
                _state_table[f"{_y}-{_c}-{_m}-{_s}"] = {
                    "v": _visibility_for(_y, _c, _m, _s),
                    "r": _yrange_for_state(_y, _c, _m, _s),
                }
_js_state = json.dumps(_state_table)
_js_y_titles = json.dumps({
    "0": "Basis, $/cwt (nominal)",
    "1": f"Basis, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
})

display(HTML(f"""
<script>
(function() {{
    var STATE = {_js_state};
    var Y_TITLES = {_js_y_titles};
    // Menu indices: 0=contract, 1=year, 2=mode, 3=show
    // (matches updatemenus order in the Python fig.update_layout)
    var current = {{ contract: 0, year: 0, mode: 0, show: 0 }};

    function findChart() {{
        // Look for a Plotly div on the page that has exactly four
        // updatemenus (our basis chart's signature) and hasn't been wired
        // by this script yet.
        var divs = document.querySelectorAll('.plotly-graph-div');
        for (var i = divs.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {{
            var d = divs[i];
            if (d && d._fullLayout && d._fullLayout.updatemenus
                && d._fullLayout.updatemenus.length === 4
                && !d.__basisToggleWired) {{
                return d;
            }}
        }}
        return null;
    }}

    function applyState(gd) {{
        var key = current.year + '-' + current.contract + '-' + current.mode + '-' + current.show;
        var s = STATE[key];
        if (!s) return;
        var yTitle = Y_TITLES[String(current.mode)];
        // annotations[6] = heifers-empty-notice; visible only in strict mode.
        // The button-args fallback already sets the right value for the
        // first click, but applyState re-asserts it here so the JS layer's
        // independent-toggle semantics keep the annotation in sync after
        // a cross-clearing reset (e.g., user toggles year while in "all"
        // mode — args temporarily set notice visible, then applyState
        // restores it to hidden because current.show is still 1).
        var noticeVisible = (current.show === 0);
        Plotly.update(gd,
            {{ visible: s.v }},
            {{
                'yaxis.range': s.r,
                'yaxis2.range': s.r,
                'yaxis.title.text': yTitle,
                'yaxis2.title.text': yTitle,
                'updatemenus[0].active': current.contract,
                'updatemenus[1].active': current.year,
                'updatemenus[2].active': current.mode,
                'updatemenus[3].active': current.show,
                'annotations[6].visible': noticeVisible,
            }}
        );
    }}

    function identifyDim(ev) {{
        // Identify which menu was clicked by button count, with a
        // label-based tiebreak for the two 2-button rows (Mode vs Show).
        // Reference comparison against gd._fullLayout.updatemenus[i]
        // does NOT work because every Plotly.update we trigger rebuilds
        // _fullLayout with new menu objects.
        //   show row     -> 2 buttons (Strict basis only / All weights & classes)
        //   mode row     -> 2 buttons (Nominal $ / Real (... $))
        //   contract row -> 3 buttons (May, November, Nearby)
        //   year row     -> 4 buttons (All, 5y, 3y, latest)
        var btns = (ev.menu && ev.menu.buttons) ? ev.menu.buttons : [];
        var nBtns = btns.length;
        if (nBtns === 4) return 'year';
        if (nBtns === 3) return 'contract';
        if (nBtns === 2) {{
            var firstLabel = (btns[0] && btns[0].label) ? String(btns[0].label) : '';
            // Mode buttons all contain "$"; Show buttons do not.
            return (firstLabel.indexOf('$') !== -1) ? 'mode' : 'show';
        }}
        return null;
    }}

    function attach() {{
        var gd = findChart();
        if (!gd) {{ setTimeout(attach, 150); return; }}
        gd.__basisToggleWired = true;

        gd.on('plotly_buttonclicked', function(ev) {{
            var dim = identifyDim(ev);
            if (!dim) return;
            var btnIdx = ev.active;
            if (typeof btnIdx !== 'number' || btnIdx < 0) {{
                btnIdx = ev.menu.buttons.indexOf(ev.button);
            }}
            current[dim] = btnIdx;
            // Defer one tick so Plotly's own button-args update fires
            // first (which momentarily clears other rows' actives via
            // the cross-clearing args), then we override with the
            // combined state.
            setTimeout(function() {{ applyState(gd); }}, 0);
        }});

        // NOTE: we deliberately do NOT call applyState(gd) on init.
        // The Python-side fig.update_layout already sets the correct
        // default visibility and active states. Calling Plotly.update
        // here would rebuild _fullLayout before the user's first click.
    }}

    if (document.readyState === 'loading') {{
        document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', attach);
    }} else {{
        attach();
    }}
}})();
</script>
"""))
```

## How to read this

**The 700–799 lb Steers panel is strict basis. The other 11 panels are weight-class spreads.** A producer hedging actual feeder-weight steers reads the blue line in the Steers panel — that's the gap between cash and futures for the population the [CME GF futures contract specification](https://www.cmegroup.com/markets/agriculture/livestock/feeder-cattle.contractSpecs.html) tracks. The other lines (300–699 lb Steers, 800–899 lb Steers, all Heifers) are cross-weight cash-futures spreads — useful for understanding the cattle cycle, for cross-hedge thinking, and for "what does a 400-lb calf trade for relative to feeder futures right now?" questions, but they are *not* a basis a producer can directly hedge with the CME GF contract. See [Methodology → Basis derivation](methodology/basis.qmd) for the formal distinction and what each kind of value lets a producer infer.

**Negative basis = cash below futures.** When the strict-basis (700–799 lb Steers) line sits below the zero reference, Clovis cash typically clears below the CME settle. A short hedger (sold futures, plans to sell cash) realizes `settle + basis` — so a $-10/cwt strict basis means the hedger receives $10 less than the futures price. Producers who hedge plan around this expected discount.

**Positive values for lighter-weight Steers and most Heifers panels are price-cycle spreads, not basis.** A 300–399 lb Steers panel showing +$50/cwt does NOT mean a producer hedges a 350-lb calf and realizes settle + $50. The panel reflects that 350-lb calf cash is structurally higher than 750-lb feeder cash (price slide) by approximately that dollar margin in the recent cattle cycle. A producer with 350-lb calves and a futures position is doing a cross-hedge whose realized basis depends on calf-vs-feeder cycle dynamics, not on the strict-basis number shown.

**The shaded band is the worst-and-best year**, not a typical year. A producer expecting "the average" should plan around the solid line; the band's edges are extremes seen once across the available years. Tight bands mean reliable; wide bands mean one or two anomaly years (drought, COVID, post-2022 cash blowout) stretched the historical range. **For non-target weight bins, expect wider bands — that's the variance of cross-weight spreads, and it's why cross-hedges are noisier than direct hedges.** The 2022 contribution to any (week, bin) cell sits on a thinner-than-typical lot count — see the [Methodology → 2022 receipts anomaly](methodology/clovis.qmd#the-2022-receipts-anomaly) section for the full discussion.

**The Nearby contract is the simplest reference.** It auto-rolls to whichever contract is closest to expiry, so it's a forward-price reference no matter what time of year a producer is selling. The May and November lines answer the more specific *"what should I expect against the spring/fall contract a producer might already have on the books?"*

## Why this view exists alongside the other charts

The chart shelf now decomposes the same Clovis-plus-CME dataset along four orthogonal axes:

| Page | Aggregates | Answers |
|---|---|---|
| [Price–weight explorer](price-weight.qmd) | across time, per (weight, class) | *what does each weight class fetch on average?* |
| [Weekly seasonality](seasonality.qmd) | across years, per (week-of-year, weight, class) | *when in the year do these prices peak?* |
| [Weekly trends](weekly-trends.qmd) | nothing — raw weekly observations | *how have prices moved week-by-week?* |
| Basis & weight-class spreads (this page) | across years, per (week-of-year, weight, class, contract) | *for 700–799 lb Steers: what's the strict hedge basis to May / November / Nearby? · for other weights and classes: what's the cash–futures spread, useful for understanding the cycle?* |

A producer who hedges 700-lb feeders, or who reads futures as a forward-price reference for the same population, gets the strict-basis answer that the cash-only views can't give. Producers and Extension peers reading the wider weight-class panels get the calf-and-yearling cycle context — which is informative even if the lines aren't a directly-hedgeable basis.

## Download

**Basis snapshot** (long-format: one row per `auction_date × class × frame × muscle_grade × weight_break × contract_month`. Only the derived `basis` is committed; the underlying CME `settle` column is computed in memory and not persisted — see [data licensing](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/LICENSE-DATA.md)):

[Parquet](../data/processed/clovis_basis_latest.parquet) · [CSV](../data/processed/clovis_basis_latest.csv) · [XLSX](../data/processed/clovis_basis_latest.xlsx)

**Manifest** (vintage stamps and SHAs for each derived basis snapshot):
[clovis_basis_MANIFEST.json](../data/processed/clovis_basis_MANIFEST.json)

Parquet is the canonical format and the smallest on disk. CSV opens in any text editor or spreadsheet app. XLSX opens directly in Excel/Numbers/LibreOffice. See the [data catalog](data.qmd) for the full inventory and per-file row counts.

The platform does not redistribute raw CME GF settle data. Producers wanting raw settles must source them directly from CME DataMine.

## See also

- [Methodology → CPI deflation](methodology/cpi.qmd) — the deflator the cash-price charts use; the same CPI series powers the basis page's real-dollar mode.
- [Methodology → Clovis pipeline](methodology/clovis.qmd) — the cash side of the basis calculation.
- [Methodology → Basis derivation](methodology/basis.qmd) — the contract-month convention, date-alignment rule, and the per-row deflation identity used by the real-dollar mode.

## Roadmap

- [ ] **Visual emphasis for the strict-basis panel.** v1 of this page surfaces the 700–799 lb Steers strict-basis call-out in prose only. v2 should add a visual signal in the chart — a thicker line, an annotation arrow, or a "show strict basis only" toggle that filters to that single panel — so a producer scanning the chart can identify their hedge basis without reading the prose first.
- [ ] **Contract-cycle aligned x-axis.** The May contract expires in late May; a producer reading basis to May cares most about the run-up *to* expiry. The agricultural-economics literature commonly aligns the basis x-axis to contract expiry rather than to the calendar — the May figure typically starts at week 22 (when the next May becomes the active reference) and wraps through week 21 of the following year, with the same logic for November (start at week 48). The current chart shows a calendar 1–52 axis for all three contracts, which is informative but not aligned to the contract cycle. Add as v2.
- [ ] **IQR (25–75 percentile) band** layered behind the min-max band — tighter "typical year" reference inside the extreme range. Activate once 5+ years of complete data have stabilized the percentile estimates.
- [ ] **Bin-isolate mode** — clicking a legend entry hides every bin except the chosen one and reveals its full band detail (per-bin focus view), without the noise of all six overlaid bands. Pairs with the strict-basis emphasis above.
- [ ] **Other contract months** (March / September / January / April / August) on demand — the data is already in the basis parquet; this is just adding more buttons to the contract row if a producer's marketing decision is keyed to a different contract.
- [ ] **A "current week marker"** showing where this week's strict basis sits against the historical average for the same week — bridge to the [Sell-now compare](sell-now-compare.qmd) page for the cash side.
